era_context: The First Mithridatic War continues with Sulla's military campaign in Greece. Rome is still fractured by civil conflict between Sullan and Marian factions. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Xuan maintains internal stability and pursues military expansion. The Eastern Mediterranean is contested between Rome, Pontus, and various Hellenistic kingdoms.

events.title: Siege of Athens concludes; Sulla captures the city
events.description: After a prolonged siege lasting approximately one year, Lucius Cornelius Sulla's forces breach the walls of Athens and capture the city from Mithridatic-aligned forces under Aristion. The siege results in significant destruction and loss of life. Sulla's capture of Athens is a major strategic victory that allows him to consolidate control over Greece and prepare for confrontation with Mithridates' main forces in Asia Minor. The city's cultural and political significance makes this a symbolic as well as military triumph.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Athens (destruction layers, inscriptions)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Athens is well-attested in ancient sources. Archaeological evidence from the Athenian Agora and other sites shows destruction consistent with siege warfare. However, some scholars debate whether the destruction layer dates to 86 or 87 BCE, and the extent of destruction attributable to Sulla versus earlier Mithridatic occupation remains contested.

events.title: Battle of Chaeronea; Sulla defeats Mithridates' general Archelaus
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla's legions decisively defeat the forces of Mithridates VI under the command of General Archelaus at Chaeronea in Boeotia. This battle is one of the largest engagements of the First Mithridatic War and demonstrates Roman military superiority over Pontic forces. The victory secures Sulla's control of Greece and forces Mithridates to consider negotiation. Archelaus survives the battle and retreats to Asia Minor.
key_figures: Mithridates VI Eupator (Pontic king, not present)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Chaeronea is one of the best-attested events of the Mithridatic Wars, mentioned in multiple ancient sources. The date of 86 BCE is widely accepted by modern scholars, though some sources place it in 87 BCE. The outcome (Roman victory) is uncontested.

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaign against the Xiongnu; expansion of control
events.description: Under Emperor Xuan, Han Dynasty forces conduct military campaigns against Xiongnu tribes in the north and northwest, consolidating Han control over the Tarim Basin and trade routes. These campaigns are part of a broader Han strategy to secure the Silk Road and establish tributary relationships with Central Asian kingdoms. The military successes strengthen Han prestige and economic power.
key_figures: Han military commanders (names not consistently recorded in surviving sources)

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu are well-documented in Chinese sources, though specific campaigns are sometimes difficult to date precisely to individual years. The broad pattern of Han expansion during Xuan's reign (74–49 BCE) is secure.
cross_references: Part of Han Dynasty expansion (206 BCE – 220 CE)
Contemporary with Roman military campaigns in the East

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Chaeronea is debated; some sources place it in 87 BCE rather than 86 BCE. The extent of Sulla's destruction in Athens has been questioned by some scholars, who argue that earlier Mithridatic occupation caused more damage. Additionally, the characterization of Archelaus as a 'general' may be imprecise—some sources suggest he held a more political role as well. The scale of Han Dynasty military campaigns is sometimes exaggerated in later Chinese sources; the actual extent of territorial control gained is debated among modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 86 BCE is heavily weighted toward Roman military history, reflecting the survival of Roman-authored texts. Greek sources are largely lost, limiting our understanding of Greek perspectives on the Mithridatic Wars. Chinese sources provide detailed documentation of Han Dynasty affairs and are generally reliable. The bias toward military and political events reflects the nature of surviving sources; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence from Greece provides material corroboration of siege and battle sites but is limited in scope.

graph_edges.note: Capture of Athens enables Sulla to consolidate forces and move against Mithridates' main army
Roman victory at Chaeronea forces Mithridates to negotiate, leading to Treaty of Dardanus
Han Dynasty military expansion occurs simultaneously with Roman military campaigns in the East
